title: ATM-Based Crevasse Detection & Extraction workflow
author: Jones-Ivey, Renette
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This workflow implements the crevasse detection with OIB/ATM Laser Altimeter Data algorithm, in parallel over a range of years and user specified operational parameters. 
 The procedure follows the analysis used to detect the morphology of sea ice features (e.g. pressure ridges) across the Arctic using OIB data (Petty et al., 2016) but applied to crevasses over specific glaciers in Greenland. Some important differences that need to be noted: 
 
 
 For sea ice we assume a relative flat reference surface (sea level) over the 500 m - 1000 m segments we analyze, whereas for these glaciers, the underlying reference surface can vary considerably. We thus experiment with fitting planes to the elevations (e.g. a quadratic plane) and assess height anomalies relative to this surface. 
 
 
 Crevasse depths can be a lot bigger than pressure ridge heights! 
 
 
 Uncertainty regarding the ability of the off-nadir conical scanning laser to accurately penetrate down to the crevasse base. 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 The crevasse detection algorithm requires NASA Airborne Topographic Mapper (ATM) laser altimetry data collected by NASA's Operation IceBridge, downloaded from  https://nsidc.org/data/ILATM1B/versions/2 , and, corresponding POS AV smoothed best estimate of trajectory (SBET) files downloaded from  https://daacdata.apps.nsidc.org/pub/DATASETS/ICEBRIDGE/IPAPP1B_GPSInsCorrected_v01 .
steps_raw:
This workflow implements the crevasse detection with OIB/ATM Laser Altimeter Data algorithm, in parallel over a range of years and user specified operational parameters. 
 The procedure follows the analysis used to detect the morphology of sea ice features (e.g. pressure ridges) across the Arctic using OIB data (Petty et al., 2016) but applied to crevasses over specific glaciers in Greenland. Some important differences that need to be noted: 
 
 
 For sea ice we assume a relative flat reference surface (sea level) over the 500 m - 1000 m segments we analyze, whereas for these glaciers, the underlying reference surface can vary considerably. We thus experiment with fitting planes to the elevations (e.g. a quadratic plane) and assess height anomalies relative to this surface. 
 
 
 Crevasse depths can be a lot bigger than pressure ridge heights! 
 
 
 Uncertainty regarding the ability of the off-nadir conical scanning laser to accurately penetrate down to the crevasse base. 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 The crevasse detection algorithm requires NASA Airborne Topographic Mapper (ATM) laser altimetry data collected by NASA's Operation IceBridge, downloaded from  https://nsidc.org/data/ILATM1B/versions/2 , and, corresponding POS AV smoothed best estimate of trajectory (SBET) files downloaded from  https://daacdata.apps.nsidc.org/pub/DATASETS/ICEBRIDGE/IPAPP1B_GPSInsCorrected_v01 .
